input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10-scale teleoperated autonomous vehicle retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, featuring lower and upper level chassis layers and autonomy elements. Operates within the Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) domain of Advanced Driver Assistance Systems (ADAS), characterized by resource-constrained electronic control units (ECUs) requiring computational offloading, real-time data monitoring, and safety-critical remote management capabilities.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: Three primary services: 1) Data Monitoring via UI for real-time vehicle state visualization, projected driving path, detected object tracks, data saving, and report generation; 2) Computation Offloading of ACC system functions (lead vehicle detection, desired speed calculation based on distance, actuation commands) to cloud resources to alleviate on-board ECU burden; 3) Remote Management enabling operators to enable/disable ACC, set speed, and issue emergency braking commands, while developers utilize data collection for troubleshooting. Targets operators for real-time control and developers for system analysis.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The system implements the ISO 23247 digital twin framework standard, utilizes Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) for middleware communication, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 SDK for hardware-accelerated sensor processing, and employs Python, C++, and MATLAB/Simulink for development, simulation, and control algorithm design.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The system requires 100 ms clock synchronization accuracy for coordination between devices to enable computation offloading in safety-critical vehicular applications, as dictated by hardware limitations.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The Digital Twin system comprises three distinct entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) each deployed on separate hardware components. Each Functional Entity (FE) is implemented as an individual ROS2 package containing one or more ROS2 nodes. The User Entity includes a singular User Interface FE split into two subcomponents (3D Visualization and Control Panel), while the Physical Twin Entity hosts resource-specific functions such as Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking, reflecting a departure from ISO 23247 RA standards.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The architecture follows ISO 23247 RA with three primary entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User) mapped to hardware components. Each Functional Entity is implemented as a ROS2 package, with inter-component coordination via ROS2 nodes. The Physical Twin Entity deviates from ISO 23247 by encompassing general automotive functionality (e.g., Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking) rather than only resource-specific OME functions, and all entities are deployed on separate hardware for system-wide coordination.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The system utilizes a Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking model that applies DBSCAN to cluster raw LiDAR point clouds and employs a Kalman filter for object tracking. The Adaptive Cruise Control (ACC) component determines the lead vehicle and computes the following speed. Data management involves storing object tracks and ego vehicle state in a SQL database.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: Physical actuators include a Vedder Electronic Speed Controller that converts desired speed into motor voltage commands. Control mechanisms via the User Interface enable/disable ACC, modify the desired speed, and initiate emergency braking.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR and Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera, collecting ego vehicle speed, steering angle, and detected objects in proximity.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Raw LiDAR point cloud data is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, with vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path transmitted in real-time. Data flows from physical sensors to the Digital Twin via ROS2 for intra-component communication and UDP over Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication. The Data Translation component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for all data streams.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands, including ACC inputs, motor actuation, and steering servo commands, are processed by the Controlling FE. The Data Translation component serializes and translates these commands, which are then transmitted via ROS2 and UDP over Wi-Fi networks. Command validation, authentication, and execution confirmation mechanisms are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The Digital Twin System utilizes ROS2 for intra-component communication within the Digital Twin entity and Wi-Fi with UDP transport layer for inter-entity communication. Requirement R7 mandates a maximum network latency of 100 ms. Bandwidth requirements, redundancy mechanisms, and security protocols are not explicitly detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: Each entity is deployed on separate hardware components, including NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano microcomputers for processing and external storage devices for data persistence. No cloud, on-premise, or edge-specific deployment model is explicitly stated beyond the hardware details.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: Compliance with ISO 23247-2:2021 reference architecture defining Observable Manufacturing Element (OME), Data Collection and Device Control Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. Functional Entities handle specific tasks such as data transformation, actuation, and user interfacing, with OPC Unified Architecture specified for interoperability implementation.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

Quotes: '1/10-scale autonomous vehicle', 'lower level chassis... upper level chassis', 'resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient'.

The description accurately lists the three primary services defined in the text: Data Monitoring (UI, visualization, reporting), Computation Offloading (ACC functions to cloud), and Remote Management (commands for operators, troubleshooting for developers). Quotes: 'UI allowing a user to view, save, and generate reports', 'offloading only the ACC system', 'Operators use the DT to monitor and remotely manage ACC'.

The extraction correctly identifies the ISO 23247 standard as the architectural framework, ROS2 for middleware, NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 for the SDK, and lists the specific programming languages and simulation tools (Python, C++, MATLAB/Simulink) along with their usage contexts. Quotes: 'fitness of the ISO 23247 RA', 'Robot Operating System 2 (ROS2) as the middleware', 'NVIDIA Jetpack 5.0 as the Software Development Kit (SDK)', 'Application modules are implemented in Python 3.8..., C++..., and MATLAB/Simulink'.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

Supported by quotes: 'Components within the DTS shall have clocks synchronized to an accuracy of 100 ms.', 'To enable computation offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.', 'We require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.'

The description correctly identifies the three entities, their hardware deployment, ROS2 implementation details, and the specific composition of the User and Physical Twin entities, including the deviation from the ISO standard. Supported by quotes: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component', 'we implement each FE as an individual ROS2 package', 'the singular User Interface FE is instantiated in the 3D Visualization and Control Panel components.'

The extraction provides a comprehensive overview of the architecture's adherence to ISO 23247 RA, the implementation via ROS2 packages, and the specific deviation regarding the Physical Twin Entity hosting general automotive functionality. Supported by quotes: 'We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design phase.', 'Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin Entity ... can host general automotive functionality'

No relevant evidence is present in the provided source documents regarding integration with external systems, interoperability standards with other Digital Twins, or enterprise system dependencies. The text focuses exclusively on the internal architecture and communication between the three specific entities (Physical Twin, Digital Twin, User).

block_3_docs_retrieved:
In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

The description accurately identifies the specific hardware (Vedder Electronic Speed Controller) and its function, as well as the remote control capabilities of the User Interface, as described in Section III-B and Table I. Quotes: 'Vedder Electronic Speed Controller', 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands.', 'enable the management and operation of ACC remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.'

The extraction correctly lists the specific sensor models (Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera) found in Section III-B and correctly identifies the data types collected (speed, steering, objects) as required by R2. Quotes: 'onboard sensors including a Hokuyo UST-10LX LiDAR, an Intel Deepsense D435i Depth Camera', 'The DTS shall collect the following ACC-relevant data in real-time: ego vehicle’s speed; ego vehicle’s steering angle; detected objects in the vehicle’s proximity.'

The extraction states 'Not in Document.' The source documents discuss architectural requirements (such as latency and clock synchronization) and general definitions of Digital Twins as 'high-fidelity computational reflections,' but they do not describe specific model fidelity requirements, validation and verification methods for the implemented system, uncertainty quantification, or error detection mechanisms. Therefore, the assessment that this specific detailed information is not in the document is correct.

(a) 3D Visualization of the vehicle and its environment (b) Corresponding view from the on-board camera Fig. 3: Visualizations available from the virtual cockpit _C. Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system. Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network using UDP in the transport layer. _1) Data Monitoring:_ To enable data monitoring, ACCrelevant data such as vehicle state information, object tracks, and projected driving path are displayed in real-time on the 3D Visualization, as shown in Fig. 3a. Raw point cloud data from the LiDAR sensor must be processed by the _Point Cloud_ _Clustering & Tracking_ SW component, which turns the raw point cloud, represented as individual points on a 2D plane,

block_4_judge.reasoning: The extracted characteristic accurately describes the data flow from physical sensors (LiDAR, vehicle state) to the Digital Twin, correctly identifying the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking component and the Data Translation component's functions. It correctly identifies ROS2 for intra-component communication and Wi-Fi for inter-entity communication. While 'UDP' is not fully visible in the truncated source text (ending in 'U'), the rest of the technical details are well-supported. Quote: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network'; 'This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions'.

The characteristic correctly identifies the flow of control commands from the Controlling FE (ACC) to the Actuation FE (motor, steering servo) and the role of the Data Translation component. It accurately uses ROS2 and Wi-Fi for the communication protocols and correctly notes that validation mechanisms are not detailed in the text. Quote: 'ACC Determines lead vehicle and computes following speed. Controlling FE'; 'Actuation Converts desired speed into motor voltage commands. Actuation FE'.

The description is highly accurate and grounded in the source text. It correctly identifies ROS2 for intra-component and Wi-Fi/UDP for inter-entity communication, and cites the 100 ms latency requirement. Sources confirm: 'Communication between SW components takes place within a ROS2 network, while inter-entity communication occurs over a Wi-Fi network' and 'The DTS shall maintain network latency of at most 100 ms'. The exclusion of bandwidth and security details is also correct.

The extraction correctly identifies the deployment on separate hardware components, specifically the NVIDIA Jetson Orin Nano, and the use of external storage. The source supports this: 'Considering that each entity has been deployed on a different hardware component' and 'The DTS shall store the ego vehicle state data on a device external to the vehicle'. The statement about the lack of explicit cloud/edge model details is accurate based on the provided text.

II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview

Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
